title: Research on the microstructure and corrosion behaviour of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel alloyed with Cu
abstract_summary: Three Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C steels with Cu contents of 0, 1, and 3 wt% were fabricated to study the influence of Cu on microstructure and corrosion properties. Adding appropriate Cu promotes austenite nucleation and growth and refines carbides, while excessive Cu reduces carbide amount. Cu does not fully dissolve but accumulates at grain boundaries, creating potential differences that synergize with carbides to cause preferential grain boundary corrosion and increased surface degradation.
research_objective: To investigate the effect of Cu content on the microstructure and corrosion behavior of Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C austenite-based low-density steel in a chloride environment.
main_process_variables: cooling: air cooling to room temperature
corrosion test medium: 3.5 wt% NaCl solution
expected_information_axis: microstructure (austenite grain size, carbide distribution, Cu segregation)
key_sections: 1. Introduction: background on low-density steels, role of alloying elements (Al, Mn, C, Cu), mechanical and corrosion property trade-offs, objective of studying Cu effect on corrosion.

2. Experimental: material preparation (melting, homogenization, hot rolling); microstructure characterization; electrochemical testing (potentiodynamic polarization, EIS, Mott-Schottky); immersion testing; scanning Kelvin probe force microscopy (SKPFM).

3. Results: microstructure and composition (carbide refinement/dissolution, Cu at grain boundaries); electrochemical behavior (polarization curves, impedance, semiconductor properties); surface morphology after immersion; SKPFM Volta potential differences.

4. Discussion: dislocation microstructures around carbides and their role in pitting initiation; analysis of corrosion products and passivation film composition; synergistic microgalvanic corrosion model involving Cu enrichment and carbides.

5. Conclusion: summary of Cu content effects on austenite growth, carbide evolution, dislocation-induced corrosion, and transition to synergistic grain-boundary corrosion mechanism causing degradation.
